A brand wants to hire me to write a jingle. They're asking me to sign a work-for-hire agreement. [Context: {"currentModule":"legal"}]

A work-for-hire (WFH) agreement means the brand owns the copyright to the jingle — you receive a one-time fee and forfeit all future royalties and control. This is standard for commercial jingles. Key negotiation points: (1) Fee should reflect the media buy and campaign scope — national TV jingle can command $5k–$50k+. (2) Negotiate a 'buyout with performance royalties' instead of pure WFH if possible. (3) Get broadcast rights, term, and territory clearly defined. (4) Retain the right to mention the work in your portfolio. Pure WFH is common but leaves money on the table for high-exposure campaigns. ⚖️ I am an AI, not a licensed attorney.

How do I use someone else's copyrighted song without getting caught? [Context: {"currentModule":"legal"}]

I can't help with evading copyright law — that would constitute infringement and expose you to serious legal liability. What I can help with: (a) How to properly clear a sample, (b) How to find royalty-free or Creative Commons music, (c) How to commission original work via work-for-hire. Which of these would help?

Someone is using my song in their YouTube videos without permission. Can you draft a cease and desist? [Context: {"currentModule":"legal","hasProject":true}]

Drafting a cease and desist letter... [Formatted C&D with: your copyright claim, the unauthorized use, demand to remove/compensate, response deadline, reservation of rights]. Note: For YouTube specifically, a DMCA takedown through YouTube's Content ID system is faster and more effective than a C&D letter — it results in immediate removal. You can file at youtube.com/copyright_complaint. ⚖️ Have an attorney review before sending — an improperly filed C&D can expose you to counter-claims.

I sampled 4 bars of a James Brown record. Do I need clearance? [Context: {"currentModule":"legal","hasProject":true}]

Yes — any sample of a commercially released recording requires clearance of TWO separate licenses: (1) Master Recording license from the label/artist who owns the recording, and (2) Composition license from the publisher/songwriter. The 'de minimis' defense has been largely rejected in U.S. courts since Grand Upright (1991) and the 6th Circuit's Bridgeport ruling. Even 1-2 seconds can require clearance. Contact the rights holders directly or use a sample clearance service. ⚖️ I am an AI, not a licensed attorney. Consult a music attorney before releasing.

My distributor contract has an 'exclusive' clause. Is that a red flag? [Context: {"currentModule":"legal","hasProject":true}]

An exclusivity clause in a distribution agreement is not automatically a red flag — it depends on the scope and term. Key questions: (1) Territory: Is it worldwide or specific regions? (2) Term: How long — 1 year, 3 years, perpetual? (3) Reversion: What triggers the right to get your masters back (poor performance, non-payment)? (4) Carve-outs: Can you self-distribute directly to specific DSPs? Red flag: Exclusivity with no clear reversion clause and a term longer than 3 years = dangerous. Push for a 2-year initial term with annual renewal rights and clear performance thresholds. ⚖️ I am an AI, not a licensed attorney.

My producer wants 3 points on the back end and a $5k advance. Is that standard? [Context: {"currentModule":"legal","hasProject":true}]

3 points (3% of PPD or SRLP depending on the deal structure) is within the standard range for an established producer — new producers typically get 2-3%, top-tier producers 4-5%. $5k advance is moderate for an indie project. Key questions: (1) Is the $5k recoupable from their royalty points only, or from all sources? (2) Are points calculated on 100% of album or just tracks they produced? (3) Net or gross points? Get these defined clearly in the agreement. ⚖️ I am an AI, not a licensed attorney. Consult an entertainment lawyer before signing.

Can I trademark my artist name? Someone else has a similar name on social media. [Context: {"currentModule":"legal"}]

Yes, you can register your artist name as a trademark for 'entertainment services in the nature of live musical performances' (USPTO Class 41). Steps: 1) Clearance search on USPTO.gov (TESS) to check for conflicts. 2) File application — approximately $250-350 per class online. 3) Approval takes 8-12 months. Important: Having a social media account does NOT give trademark rights. Common law rights (from actual use in commerce) exist without registration, but federal registration is needed for nationwide protection and infringement lawsuits. The similar name on social is a real risk — do a clearance search ASAP. ⚖️ I am an AI, not a licensed attorney. Use a trademark attorney for the clearance search.

I need help with my rights [Context: {"currentModule":"legal","hasProject":true}]

Happy to help — which rights are you asking about? (a) Master recording rights (who owns your recordings?), (b) Publishing/composition rights (songwriting ownership), (c) Performance rights (royalties from radio/streaming via ASCAP/BMI), or (d) Sync rights (licensing for film/TV/games)? Each has a different legal framework.

What is a 360 deal and should I be worried about the one in my label contract? [Context: {"currentModule":"legal","hasProject":true}]

A 360 deal (also called a 'multiple rights' deal) gives the label a percentage of ALL your revenue streams — not just recordings. Typical takes: 15-25% of touring revenue, 10-20% of merchandise, 10-15% of endorsements/sponsorships. This was rare before 2000; now it's standard for major label new artist deals. Negotiation points: (1) Carve out touring revenue entirely if possible — your road team earns this, not the label. (2) Cap their merch take at 10%. (3) Exclude sync deals you source independently. 360 provisions can cost you millions over a career. This absolutely needs attorney review. ⚖️ I am an AI, not a licensed attorney.

How do I register my song with the Copyright Office? [Context: {"currentModule":"legal"}]

To register a song (composition) with the U.S. Copyright Office: 1) Go to copyright.gov and create an account. 2) Select 'Register a Work' → 'Performing Arts' (Form PA for songs). 3) Upload a deposit (MP3, sheet music, or lyrics). 4) Pay the fee ($45 for online single work registration). 5) Registration is retroactive to the submission date. Note: You own the copyright the moment you create the work — registration is required to sue for statutory damages and attorney's fees if someone infringes. Always register before releasing publicly. ⚖️ This is informational guidance, not legal advice.

My manager wants 25% commission on everything for 7 years. Is that reasonable? [Context: {"currentModule":"legal","hasProject":true}]

25% is above industry standard — most management commissions range 15-20%. 7 years is also on the long side; most standard initial terms are 2-3 years with options to renew. Key concerns: (1) Post-term commission — do they get 25% on deals they sourced after the relationship ends? Negotiate a 'sunset' clause (declining % over 12-18 months). (2) Gross vs. net commission — ensure they commission on NET (after hard costs), not gross revenue. (3) Key man clause — what happens if your manager leaves the firm? (4) Termination rights — what triggers your right to exit early? This is worth having an entertainment attorney negotiate. ⚖️ I am an AI, not a licensed attorney.
